title: The Deposition

jokes.text: Every time Michael's in a meeting, he makes me come in and give him a post-it note telling him who's on the phone. I did it once, and he freaked out. He loved it so much. The thing is, he doesn't get that many calls. So he has me make them up every ten minutes.
jokes.setup: Michael needs to appear important in meetings
jokes.punchline: He doesn't get many calls, so Pam has to invent fake ones every ten minutes
jokes.explanation: Perfect character-driven humor that reveals Michael's insecurity and Pam's resigned enabling. The escalation from one incident to a regular charade is beautifully crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Pam, I need you to call me during meetings. Make it sound important.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: How often?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Every ten minutes or so.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Michael, you don't get any calls. I'm going to have to invent fake ones.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know. Just make them sound real.

jokes.text: But I have the most important client sitting right in front of me... my boss.
jokes.setup: Michael claims to prioritize an important client call
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's calling Ryan 'the most important client' as brownnosing
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael sycophantic behavior, well-integrated with character but not groundbreaking

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I need to take this call. It's my most important client.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Ryan? Hey buddy, it's Michael. Just checking in on you.

jokes.text: My house, my rules... I insist. I insist you take your work calls.
jokes.setup: Michael refuses to let Ryan take a call, claiming authority
jokes.punchline: Immediately reverses position while maintaining the authoritative tone
jokes.explanation: Shows Michael's need to feel in control even when capitulating

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Ryan, you cannot take that call. I am your boss and I forbid it.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? Take the call. But take it in the conference room. And close the door quietly.

jokes.text: The company fired her for having the courage to augment her boobs.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining Jan's lawsuit
jokes.punchline: Frames breast augmentation as an act of 'courage'
jokes.explanation: Michael's twisted logic and inappropriate phrasing perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jan is suing the company for sexual harassment. But here's the thing - she got breast augmentation, which I think shows a lot of courage. I mean, that's not something you do for a man, that's something you do for yourself.

jokes.text: Pat-tern. Patt-ern. My friend friend Pat took a turn. That's how I remember that.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to memorize legal terminology
jokes.punchline: Creates nonsensical mnemonic device involving 'friend Pat took a turn'
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly stupid Michael logic, creates memorable absurd imagery

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, so I need to remember these legal terms. Let me create a mnemonic device. Friend Pat Took A Turn. F-P-T-A-T. See? Easy to remember.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That doesn't spell anything, Michael.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It spells Friend Pat Took A Turn, Dwight. Come on, keep up.

jokes.text: No, I want it up. My hair.
jokes.setup: Michael wants convertible top down because he's queasy
jokes.punchline: Jan's vain priority of protecting her hair over Michael's comfort
jokes.explanation: Efficient character moment showing Jan's vanity vs Michael's needs

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jan, can you put the top down? I'm getting a little queasy.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: No, Michael. I just had my hair done.

jokes.text: Disree. My friend Disree got new specs. Disree spect. My friend Inapro drives a Prius with his behind neighbor.
jokes.setup: Continuing the mnemonic device pattern
jokes.punchline: Even more nonsensical memory aids, ending with 'behind neighbor' for inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Escalates the absurdity beautifully, 'behind neighbor' is wonderfully weird

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I got another one. R-A-P-E... Radishes Are Pretty Excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No wait, that doesn't work. Um, uh... Rarely Are People Eating.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And then for B-N... Behind Neighbor. Wait, no, that sounds—

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what, forget it.

jokes.text: That's 4 million. $4 million! Man! That is a lot of guacamole. Lot of green. Lotta green.
jokes.setup: Michael excited about lawsuit money
jokes.punchline: Reduces $4 million to 'lot of guacamole' then catches himself with 'lotta green'
jokes.explanation: Michael's childlike reduction of huge sums to food metaphors

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: With four million dollars, I could buy a lot of guacamole.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I mean, a lotta green.

jokes.text: And I've also thrown in some ers and ahs just to make it seem like it's not memorized. Perfect crime.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining his testimony preparation
jokes.punchline: Calls obvious deception a 'perfect crime' in a talking head
jokes.explanation: Michael's delusional confidence in transparent schemes is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I've been preparing for my testimony. I've memorized key facts, practiced my delivery, really thought through my answers.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: The key is to commit to the lie completely. No hesitation, no second-guessing. That's what makes it a perfect crime.

jokes.text: I'm gonna puke. I'm gonna throw up. Fine, just a second. Hold on.
jokes.setup: Michael has been asking to put the top down
jokes.punchline: Jan only caves when he threatens to vomit in her car
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy payoff to the convertible top setup

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: If you don't put the top down, I'm going to vomit in your car.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Oh my God, fine!

jokes.text: A good start. And I think it's 'busload.' Yeah, but rich lawyers took the bus.
jokes.setup: Classic lawyer joke about busload driving off cliff
jokes.punchline: Michael's correction and misunderstanding about rich lawyers
jokes.explanation: Generic joke setup with Michael's typical misunderstanding as mild twist

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: A busload of lawyers drove off a cliff. At the bottom, one lawyer survived. He looked up and said, 'Thank God for the one honest lawyer in the bunch.'

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's not how that joke goes. The lawyer at the bottom says, 'Well, that's one less lawyer.' And then he's sad because he made a lot of money off those guys.

jokes.text: My boyfriend is 12.
jokes.setup: Pam explains Jim plays ping-pong and she brings him juice
jokes.punchline: Reduces their relationship dynamic to childish behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and self-awareness from Pam about the absurd situation

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim plays ping-pong, and I bring him juice.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: We're basically in an adult relationship now.

jokes.text: I don't talk trash, I talk smack. They're totally different. Trash talk is all hypothetical, like, 'Your mom is so fat, she could eat internet.' But smack talk is happening, like, right now. Like, 'You're ugly, and I know it for a fact, 'cause I got the evidence right there.'
jokes.setup: Kelly explains difference between trash talk and smack talk
jokes.punchline: Absurd examples including 'eat internet' and direct insult to cameraman
jokes.explanation: Pure Kelly - overconfident explanation with hilariously absurd examples and meta humor

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Okay, so trash talk is when you talk trash about someone, like 'Your mama's so fat...' But smack talk is when you talk smack about someone, like 'Your face is so ugly...'

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Like, you could say 'I hope you step on a Lego' or 'I hope you eat internet.'

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: And you, cameraman, you look like a thumb.

jokes.text: Your boyfriend is so weak, he needs steroids just to watch baseball. Jim couldn't hit a ping-pong ball of the size of the moon. Were Jim's parents first cousins that were also bad at ping-pong?
jokes.setup: Examples of Kelly's trash talk about Jim
jokes.punchline: Escalating absurdity ending with oddly specific incest-ping-pong combo
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation with the last insult being wonderfully specific and weird

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Jim? More like Jim-othy the Lame. His face looks like a foot had a baby with a thumb.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: And his hair? It's not even a real style. It's just what happens when you don't care about anything, which is basically Jim's whole personality.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: He probably sleeps with his socks on. Both feet AND his hands, because he has no idea what normal people do.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Jim probably makes out with his cousin at family reunions while they play ping-pong. That's his idea of romance.

jokes.text: Yesh.
jokes.setup: Michael being sworn in for deposition
jokes.punchline: Nervous 'yes' comes out as Sean Connery-esque 'yesh'
jokes.explanation: Simple nervous verbal tick that's very Michael under pressure

dialogue.speaker: Court Official
dialogue.line: Do you swear to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yesh.

jokes.text: People underestimate Michael. There are plenty of things that he is well above average at. Like... ice skating. He is a very good ice skater.
jokes.setup: Jan defending Michael's competence
jokes.punchline: Long pause before naming ice skating as his main skill
jokes.explanation: Perfect pause comedy and hilariously specific/irrelevant skill to defend

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Michael is an excellent manager. He's great with people, he's decisive, he's...

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: ...he's really good at ice skating.

jokes.text: I haven't actually seen it. But I have seen The Firm, and I plan on renting The Pelican Brief.
jokes.setup: Asked how long he's known 'the plaintiff'
jokes.punchline: Thinks plaintiff means a movie, references legal thrillers instead
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misunderstanding that's perfectly Michael - legal ignorance + movie knowledge

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh, the plaintiff? I've known about them for... I mean, is that like a Denzel Washington movie? Because I've seen all those legal thrillers. Philadelphia, A Few Good Men... I'm pretty sure I know what I'm talking about here.

jokes.text: That's what she said.
jokes.setup: Lawyer asks 'you were directly under her the entire time?'
jokes.punchline: Michael's signature catchphrase at inappropriate legal moment
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael catchphrase in worst possible context - perfect setup from lawyer

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's what she said.

jokes.text: Come again? That's what she said.
jokes.setup: Lawyer asks him to repeat his statement
jokes.punchline: Michael does it again with 'come again'
jokes.explanation: Escalation of the running gag - Michael can't help himself even after being told to stop

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Can you repeat your statement?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Can you repeat your statement?

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: No, your statement.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Your statement.

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Come again?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Come again.

jokes.text: Delivery's all wrong. She's butchering it.
jokes.setup: Lawyer reads back his 'that's what she said' delivery
jokes.punchline: Michael critiques her comedic timing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary - Michael as comedy critic in his own deposition

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: That's what she said.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You gotta pause before the punchline. That's what... she said. The pause is what makes it funny.

jokes.text: To be delicate, they hang off milady's chest. They... make milk.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to delicately reference Jan's breasts
jokes.punchline: Awkward euphemisms ending with clinical 'make milk'
jokes.explanation: Michael's attempt at delicacy makes it more awkward - classic cringe comedy

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jan, you have... those... um... well, they're very... perky. Like two... round... assets. Really top-notch... upper... chest... situation. I mean, they really... you know, they could practically... make milk.

jokes.text: And, frankly, the timing was nothing short of predominant.
jokes.setup: Michael commenting on timing of Jan's firing after surgery
jokes.punchline: Uses 'predominant' instead of a word like 'suspicious' or 'coincidental'
jokes.explanation: Michael's malapropism - trying to sound legal and sophisticated but choosing wrong word

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's pretty predominant that Jan got fired right after her surgery.

jokes.text: Well, depends on how you define 'began.' If it was from the first time we shook hands, it's like six years ago. The first time we kissed, it's like two years ago. From the first time we kissed sober, it was four months after that.
jokes.setup: Asked when relationship with Jan began
jokes.punchline: Reveals drunk kissing two years before official relationship
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation with the 'sober' revelation being the devastating kicker

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, technically, it started two years ago when we were both drunk at the Christmas party and made out in the parking lot.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Michael, we agreed never to talk about that.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Right, right. So, officially, it started last year.

jokes.text: Mr. Scott, do you realize you just contradicted yourself? I did? Yes. Can I go to the bathroom? I really have to. I've been drinking lots of water. You went 5 minutes ago. It wasn't to go to the bathroom. but to get out of a question. You've to answer it. First, can I go to the bathroom? No.
jokes.setup: Michael caught in contradiction, asks for his rehearsed lines
jokes.punchline: Reveals the previous bathroom break was to avoid questions
jokes.explanation: Michael's desperation and transparent avoidance tactics perfectly executed

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Can I get my card back? I need to see what I'm supposed to say here.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh right, right. Um... I was in the bathroom.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, so I WAS in the bathroom. But that was to avoid these questions.

jokes.text: All of my heroes are table tennis players. Zoran Primorac, Jan-Ove Waldner, Wong Tao, Jorg Rosskopf, and, of course, Ashraf Helmy. I even have a life-size poster of Hugo Hoyama on my wall. And the first time I left Pennsylvania was to go to the hall of fame induction ceremony of Andrzej Grubba.
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining his ping-pong expertise
jokes.punchline: Extensive knowledge of obscure ping-pong players and devoted fandom
jokes.explanation: Pure Dwight - obsessive knowledge of niche subject with earnest delivery

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I've been playing competitive ping-pong since I was four years old. I know every player on the professional circuit. I can tell you their strengths, their weaknesses, their personal lives. I once drove eight hours to watch a regional tournament in Pennsylvania just to see Soren Larsen play. Most people don't even know who Soren Larsen is. I do. I know everything about him.

jokes.text: Just got back from Jamaica. Tan almost everywhere. Jan almost everywhere. Hee hee. Oh, diary, what a week. I had sex with my boss. I don't know if it's going to go anywhere. Jan was very specific that this is not going anywhere. That it was a one-time mistake. But we had sex six times, so you tell me. I am definitely feeling very eerie.
jokes.setup: Michael's diary being read aloud in legal deposition
jokes.punchline: Intimate details including 'six times' contradiction and 'feeling very eerie'
jokes.explanation: Devastating cringe comedy - private intimate thoughts exposed in legal setting with perfect escalation

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: According to your diary entry from March 15th, you state that you and your partner were intimate six times that evening.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's... that's not accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: So you're denying the diary entry?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, I'm denying the six times. It was seven.

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: And you also wrote that you were 'feeling very eerie' afterward. Can you explain that?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That was a typo. I meant 'weary.'

jokes.text: Irie. Irie, sorry.
jokes.setup: Lawyer mispronounces 'irie' as 'eerie'
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects pronunciation of Jamaican slang mid-deposition
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael moment - correcting Jamaican slang while his intimate diary is being read

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's pronounced 'irie', not 'eerie'. It's Jamaican slang. It means everything is good, everything is cool.

jokes.text: Could you make it 11?
jokes.setup: Lawyer says they'll make 10 copies of Michael's diary
jokes.punchline: Michael asks for an extra copy for himself
jokes.explanation: Michael's obliviousness to how this looks - wants a copy of his own embarrassing diary

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: We'll make 10 copies of your diary.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Can you make it 11? I want one for myself.

jokes.text: When I was a kid, my parents got divorced. They both wanted custody. And they both asked me to testify against the other one in court. So, I don't know. I didn't want them to get divorced in the first place. I loved them both so much. I just wanted...
jokes.setup: Jim trying to comfort Michael during break
jokes.punchline: Reveals traumatic childhood parallel to current situation
jokes.explanation: Darker character moment for Jim, shows his empathy and adds depth to the comedy

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hey, Michael, I know this is tough right now.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You don't understand, Jim. This is the worst thing that's ever happened to me.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What do you mean? What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: When I was a kid, my dad left me at a Sandals Jamaica resort. Alone. For three days.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's... wow, that's terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And that's exactly what's happening now. Except this time, the resort is Dunder Mifflin.

jokes.text: You e-mailed a topless photo of me to everyone in our company. Let's call it even.
jokes.setup: Jan upset about diary being shared
jokes.punchline: Reveals Michael sent her topless photo to whole company
jokes.explanation: Shocking escalation that reframes their relationship dynamic - both are terrible

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: You shared my diary with the whole office?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jan, I can explain...

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: And you sent that photo to everyone too?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It was a mistake, it was an accident.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: An accident? Michael, that was a topless photo!

jokes.text: Not a woman, just a cool, great-looking best friend.
jokes.setup: Asked about Ryan being 'just as hot as Jan'
jokes.punchline: Michael's denial while describing Ryan as 'great-looking'
jokes.explanation: Michael's transparent attraction to Ryan while trying to deny it

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, no, no. Ryan is not as hot as Jan. Ryan is great-looking, don't get me wrong, but Jan is a woman and Ryan is... Ryan.

jokes.text: I'm out of carrots. I'm out of sticks. Mr. Scott has time and again proven himself to be an unmanageable employee and a poor branch manager. I recommend he be removed from that position and reassigned to sales, where he belongs.
jokes.setup: Michael reading Jan's negative performance review
jokes.punchline: Devastating assessment read by Michael himself, starting with 'carrots and sticks'
jokes.explanation: Brutal irony of Michael reading his own professional destruction aloud

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, um... 'Jan Levinson-Gould has no business acumen whatsoever. She is merely a figurehead for the Dunder Mifflin Scranton branch. She has no leadership skills, and her management style is completely ineffective. She relies too heavily on carrots and sticks, and not enough on... positive reinforcement and the human touch.'

jokes.text: Well, he has gone from completely hopeless to simply miserable.
jokes.setup: Asked about Jim's ping-pong progress
jokes.punchline: Dwight's backhanded assessment of improvement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight delivery - technically positive while still insulting

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Jim has improved significantly. He's gone from being completely terrible to just being regular terrible.

jokes.text: Oh, no, no. He works here, dumb-ass.
jokes.setup: Jim thinks he's preparing to play an important client
jokes.punchline: Reveals Darryl is just a warehouse employee, insults Jim
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection payoff - Jim's been training for nothing special

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So this is a really important client meeting?

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Nah man, I work in the warehouse. You thought you were gonna close some big deal with a warehouse guy? Come on, Jim. You're not that good at your job.

jokes.text: See? I was number one contender. I was being groomed.
jokes.setup: Michael hearing he was considered for Jan's job
jokes.punchline: Misinterprets being 'considered' as being the top choice
jokes.explanation: Michael's delusional interpretation before the crushing revelation

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, so I'm getting Jan's job?

dialogue.speaker: HR
dialogue.line: No, you were just one of the candidates they considered.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Right, so I'm getting Jan's job. That's what 'considered' means. They considered me, and now I have it.

dialogue.speaker: HR
dialogue.line: No, Michael, they considered several people.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Exactly! And they're going to choose me. I'm the most considerate choice.

jokes.text: No.
jokes.setup: Asked if Michael was seriously considered for corporate job
jokes.punchline: Simple, brutal 'no' after all the buildup
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - single word destroys Michael's delusions completely

dialogue.speaker: David Wallace
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Hey, hey, you, you, I don't like your boyfriend, 'Cause, 'cause, 'cause he sucks at ping-pong.
jokes.setup: Kelly singing during ping-pong game
jokes.punchline: Parodies Avril Lavigne song with ping-pong lyrics
jokes.explanation: Typical Kelly pop culture reference adapted to current situation

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Why'd you have to go and make things so complicated? It's not fair to the ball!

jokes.text: I think you're a nice guy too.
jokes.setup: David Wallace apologizing to Michael after deposition
jokes.punchline: Michael throws Wallace's dismissive 'nice guy' comment back at him
jokes.explanation: Subtle but effective callback - Michael gets a rare moment of dignity

dialogue.speaker: David Wallace
dialogue.line: Michael, I want to apologize. I know things have been difficult, but I respect you.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Oh, so now I'm a nice guy? Because last week during the deposition you said I was a 'nice guy' like it was a bad thing. Like being nice was somehow a flaw. Well, you know what? I am a nice guy. And I'm proud of it.

jokes.text: But she already brought the diary with her to New York. So... You expect to get screwed by your company. But you never expect to get screwed by your girlfriend.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining why he testified against Jan
jokes.punchline: Realizes Jan planned to betray him all along
jokes.explanation: Dark but poignant realization - shows Michael isn't completely clueless

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I testified against Jan because she was going to betray me anyway. She had this whole plan, and I was just... I was just a pawn in her game.

jokes.text: Chinese was my cheap suggestion.
jokes.setup: Jan and Michael arguing about dinner, trying to save money
jokes.punchline: Jan defends Chinese food as her 'cheap' option
jokes.explanation: Shows their financial stress and petty arguing over small expenses

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jan, we need to cut back on expenses. We can't keep eating out like this.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Chinese food is cheap.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Cheap? We spent forty dollars last night alone.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: That's cheap for us.

tmdb_overview: Michael is driving to New York with Jan where he is going to speak at her deposition. Jan is suing Dunder Mifflin for four million dollars over her wrongful termination. Michael is excited to testify and has memorized every answer Jan told him to.
insight: Courtroom setting sustains 84-point comedy through 40 tight jokes in tense deposition sequences.
